data.title: Perrigo Company: Signs Speak Caution
data.description:
Uncertain Prospects For Perrigo Company Shares

The uncertainty regarding future prospects is still reflected in the share price of Perrigo Company plc (PRGO), as was the case at the time of our previous article on October 20, 2022, and we reiterate our cautious Hold signal.

In line with this assessment and the opinion of Chris Schott, senior analyst at JPMorgan in the US, published by Seeking Alpha on November 6, 2025, the company's profitability (a key factor in US stock market sentiment) will continue to be closely monitored, three years after our previous article. Schott will revise his forecast based on future developments in the baby food industry and consumption patterns in the over-the-counter market. Based on the third-quarter 2025 results, these markets still showed weakness for Perrigo.

Our Perspective on Perrigo In This Update

From our perspective, a neutral “Hold” rating, which indicates caution and a wait-and-see attitude, is a sensible course of action, as explored in this analysis. As the saying goes, "A bird in the hand is worth ten in the air", and this "hold" rating doesn't preclude an even more cautious approach, should one prefer to focus on what can still be saved or repaired, rather than risk remaining mired in uncertainty about the future. ​​After all, markets are vast and diverse, and these characteristics offer opportunities for recovery through diversification into other companies or even sectors.

Deterioration In Market Sentiment: Amid Recent Events

The following has dampened the pessimistic market sentiment: Perrigo is facing a class-action lawsuit (deadline: January 16, 2026) for securities fraud in connection with its $170 million acquisition of Nestlé's infant formula plant and North American trademark rights in November 2022. Investors accuse the company of misleading the market by failing to disclose certain operational problems. The company had to announce unexpected expenses that surprised the market. Also, it seems that the market had built up much higher expectations for sales, profit margins, and earnings. When these were factored into the situation presented by Perrigo, the disappointment led to a deterioration in sentiment, which dealt a substantial blow to the share price.

From Medium to Small: Necessity Can Transform into Virtue for Volatility-Resistant Investors

In late 2025, Perrigo Company (NYSE ticker PRGO) was removed from the S&P MidCap 400 Index and added to the S&P SmallCap 600 Index. While this may appear to be a downgrade of Perrigo's rating, it may help to clarify the picture of this stock, as the number of companies in this prestigious benchmark index is relevant. It's possible that large investors with access to more granular information and in-depth analysis now view Perrigo as a less established healthcare company than they once did. At the same time, it's also conceivable that well-informed large investors might realign their portfolios if the economic landscape ahead turns in a way that appeals to smaller, more passionate growth stocks. This realignment would be more in line with the assumption that these stocks are better able to withstand volatility and may even have opportunities, potentially giving the “Hold” rating a better chance of recovery than commonly believed.

Our Previous Coverage of Perrigo Company plc

Given the uncertainties in the stock market and concerns that inflation could erode profit margins, we expected Perrigo's stock to face similar challenges, driving investors toward safer investments such as bonds. At the time, Perrigo had been restructuring its over-the-counter [OTC] products in the US and abroad, and its acquisition of Héra SAS (HRA Pharma) in 2021 aimed to strengthen its position in the European market. While this acquisition was initially positive, it did impact profitability. Despite this, Perrigo continued to pay dividends, demonstrating its commitment to shareholders.

Despite high debt levels, the balance sheet looked stable, and there were signs of recovery about to emerge, but the outlook remained uncertain, particularly due to the looming economic recession. An economic recession could reduce spending on non-essential, over-the-counter products such as skin care products and nutritional supplements. Some investors expressed concerns.

Given the uncertainty surrounding the economic outlook and potential short-term decline in share prices, we indicated a wait-and-see approach: how the company's profitability develops until positive results emerge.

Perrigo Company plc as of Q3 2025: Year-On-Year Changes

The situation, as stated directly by the company for the reporting period Q3-2025, is as follows.

Seeking Alpha reported:

 Perrigo press release (PRGO): Q3 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.80 beats by $0.03.

 Revenue of $1.04B (-5.5% Y/Y) misses by $60M.

Net sales declined by 4.1% due to organic sales dropping 4.4%, primarily driven by weakness in the over-the-counter [OTC] global market and challenges in the infant formula market.

In its Q3 2025 financial report, the company stated:

Organic Net Sales: Down due to -2.8% from businesses under strategic review (both Infant Formula and Oral Care, which was previously announced at the company's February 2025 Investor Day). An additional impact of -1.6% was from global OTC businesses, where soft total OTC category consumption was partially offset by store brand market share gains and gains in key brands.

Situation by Segment: Sales Down

Perrigo's CSCA and CSCI segments both experienced a decline in sales. The Americas segment decreased by 3.8%, with growth in the OTC segment offset by lower sales in strategically reviewed business areas (infant nutrition and oral care). The international segment declined by 4.5%, driven by sales decreases in respiratory health, vitamin supplements, oral care, and women's health. However, market share gains in key brands partially offset this decline.

Outlook for Perrigo's Sales and Earnings Amid Portfolio Valuation and Potential Divestments

Perrigo has updated its forecast for the financial year 2025 and adjusted expectations due to market dynamics in the infant nutrition sector and weak OTC trends, according to its report for the third quarter of 2025.

The company stated:

Reported net sales growth of -2.5% to -3.0%. Organic net sales growth of -2.0% to -2.5%. Adjusted gross margin of approximately 39%. Adjusted operating margin of approximately 15%. Interest expense of approximately $155 million. Adjusted effective tax rate of approximately 18.5%. Adjusted diluted EPS range of $2.70 to $2.80, equating to growth of 5% to 9%. Adjusted weighted average shares outstanding of approximately 138.5 million. Net leverage of approximately 3.8x adjusted EBITDA.

Perrigo's “Project Energize” program optimizes operations, reduces unnecessary costs, ultimately positions the company for more consistent and efficient growth, aiming at $140 million to $170 million in annual savings by the end of 2026.

Cash Flow And Its Allocation

Operating cash flow for the first 3 quarters of calendar year 2025 was $63 million, an increase of 25.5% year-over-year. Cash inflows of $128 million in the second and third quarters offset the cash outflow of $65 million in the first quarter. The company stated:

[…] first quarter cash outflow was driven by cash utilized to rebuild inventory in the infant formula business, settle outstanding litigation and cover Project Energize restructuring costs.

In the first 9 months of calendar 2025, the company has allocated $67 million for investments, less compared to the approximately $81 million allocated during the same period in 2024. At the same time, proceeds from divestitures in the first nine months of 2025 amounted to $14.4 million, compared to $205.5 million from the sale of the Rare Diseases business unit in the first nine months of 2024.

In the first nine months of 2025, $26.1 million in debt was repaid, compared to $420.4 million in the same period of the previous year. The company did not incur any new debt in the first nine months of 2025, compared to $1,092.7 million in the same period of the previous year.

Perrigo distributed a total of $119.4 million in dividends in the first nine months of 2025, compared to $112.9 million in the previous year. This reflects the company's strong financial position despite declining profitability.

The company is taking on less debt financing, selling non-core products from its portfolio and hopes to help margins through cost savings and optimization.

The Balance Sheet

The company's balance sheet for the end of the third quarter of 2025 shows the following figures:

  • Cash and cash equivalents amounted to US$432 million, less than the US$558.8 million as of December 30, 2024, and sharply less than the US$1,462.4 million as of September 30, 2024.
  • Total debt was US$3.64 billion, which is roughly the same level as at the end of 2024, but represents a significant reduction from the total debt of nearly US$5 billion as of September 30, 2024.

Existing liquid assets will be increased by at least Euro 300 million (or US$356.79 million at the current EUR/USD exchange rate) if the sale of Perrigo's dermocosmetics brand business to Kairos Bidco, an investment firm managed by KKR, for up to Euro 327 million is completed by the first quarter of 2026.

Balance Sheet Under Debt Ratio Test

According to figures from Seeking Alpha,

Net Debt-to-LTM EBITDA ratio

Perrigo Company plc has a market capitalization of $$2.01 billion and a current net debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 4.31. For comparison:

  • Church & Dwight Co. (CHD) Inc. has a market capitalization of $24.12 billion and a ratio of 1.3.
  • Herbalife Ltd. (HLF) has a market capitalization of $1.71 billion and a ratio of 3.1.
  • Central Garden & Pet Company (CENT) has a market capitalization of $2.13 billion and a ratio of 2.04.
  • Nature's Sunshine Products, Inc. (NATR) has a market capitalization of $457.33 million, net cash position (instead of a net debt position) of $81.3 million, and a twelve-month EBITDA of $38.5 million.

LTM Interest Coverage Ratio

Perrigo Company plc has an interest coverage ratio (ICR) (= EBIT of the last twelve months / interest expense of the last twelve months) of 2.34x. For comparison:

  • Church & Dwight Co. Inc. had zero interest expense in the twelve months ending in December 2025.
  • Herbalife Ltd. has a ratio of 2.3.
  • Central Garden & Pet Company has a ratio of 4.55.
  • Nature's Sunshine Products, Inc. has a ratio of 240.

Altman Z-Score

Perrigo Company plc has an Altman Z-score of 0.84.

Seeking Alpha explains:

The Altman Z Score may be used to predict the probability that a firm will go into bankruptcy within two years. It uses profitability, leverage, liquidity, solvency, and activity to predict whether a company has a high probability of becoming insolvent. A score below 1.8 means it's likely the company is headed for bankruptcy, while companies with scores above 3 are not likely to go bankrupt.

For comparison:

  • Church & Dwight Co. Inc. has a score of 5.14.
  • Herbalife Ltd. has a score of 2.30.
  • Central Garden & Pet Company has a score of 2.53.
  • Nature's Sunshine Products, Inc. has a score of 4.57.

Compared to financially stronger competitors, Perrigo's financial risk is considerable due to its high, albeit still economically manageable, debt level.

The very low Altman Z-score indicates significant financial difficulties and an increased risk of insolvency. This situation reinforces our cautious assessment of Perrigo, particularly for investors who cannot tolerate the risk of volatility and the associated uncertainty at this time. If this uncertainty is not effectively addressed now, it could further jeopardize the company's long-term solvency.

PRGO Perrigo Company plc on the NYSE

Perrigo Company plc’s NYSE-listed shares under the symbol “PRGO” were trading at $14.34/share, giving it a market capitalization of $2.01 billion, as of the writing of the article. The stock offers a Div Rate [FWD] of $1.16 or a Yield [FWD] of 7.95%.

Technical Analysis

The current share price is 33.5% below the 52-week middle point of $21.55 per share, and the 52-week range is $12.17 to $30.93 per share.

The 20-day moving average is slightly above the 50-day moving average, which could indicate that market participants are less pessimistic in the short term—although this could be short-lived. Actually, short-term trends remain well below the 200-day moving average, suggesting that market sentiment is likely to remain bearish for the time being until Perrigo's overall situation is further clarified.

Valuation: NOT Synonymous With Cheap Stock

Perrigo's valuation is below the sector median, but not because Perrigo is a cheap opportunity, but because its market valuation has been deteriorating for the past couple of years, and the future requires a brighter outlook than it currently looks.

But the Continued Dividend Is A Fact So Far

Perrigo’s continues to pay a quarterly dividend of $0.29 per share. The last payment was made on December 16, 2025. This marks 22 consecutive years of dividend payments, compared to a sector median of 16.3 years.

Perrigo has increased its dividend:

  • The trailing twelve-month dividend growth rate (TTM) is +7.68% compared to a sector median of +4.59%.
  • The compound annual growth rate [CAGR] over the past three years is +3.71% compared to a sector median of +5.64%.
  • The compound annual growth rate [CAGR] over the past five years is +5.21% compared to a sector median of +6.36%.
  • The compound annual growth rate [CAGR] over the past ten years is +8.78%, compared to a sector median of +6.99%.

Regarding the PRGO Dividend Safety Grade, Seeking Alpha assigns it a B rating, a green light, while those holding the stock in their portfolio are rightly hoping for a general improvement across the board at Perrigo.

The Risks

Persistent Bearish Market Sentiment

The first risk to holding a position is that market sentiment could remain negative until Perrigo's overall position becomes clearer. And it could become a problem if this negative sentiment persists for too long, as Perrigo is no longer included in the mid-cap stock index, but in the small-cap index. While the two categories have performed similarly over the past 12 months, mid-caps generally outperform small-caps over the long term.

Consider the following ETFs as benchmarks, for example. The State Street SPDR S&P MidCap 400 ETF Trust (MDY) has returned +39.51% over the past five years, compared to the Vanguard S&P Small-Cap 600 Index Fund ETF (VIOO), which returned +23% over the same period.

Market Sentiment Challenges

For a bull market in small-cap stocks to occur, conditions must change, and continued interest rate cuts by the Federal Reserve would contribute to this. Small-cap stocks require robust investment at the lowest possible interest rates. But several factors complicate the situation:

A) After three consecutive rate cuts last year, the Fed left the federal funds rate unchanged at its target range of 3.5%-3.75% at its January 2026 meeting, in line with expectations. Chairman Powell indicated during his regular press conference that the U.S. economy is in good shape and that the Fed's objectives can be achieved at current interest rates. This translated: interest rates can remain unchanged for as long as the Fed deems it practicable.

B) After months of speculation, US President Donald Trump will nominate Kevin Warsh as the next Chairman of the Federal Reserve. This development is being closely watched in light of the ongoing conflict between Trump and current Fed Chairman Jerome Powell. The US president argues that the current Fed chairman should have lowered interest rates long ago to stimulate US growth. A contradiction appears to be emerging, as Warsh is not known as an advocate for low-interest rates, and many on Wall Street view him as an inflation hawk. He advocates for a policy of higher interest rates, preventing the Fed from voluntarily losing control of prices.

C) Even if interest rates were to be cut, which is typically beneficial for rapid-growing companies, many of which operate in the small-cap sector, Perrigo does not currently exhibit the characteristics of these companies. Moreover, lenders will likely be reluctant to extend more credit under current circumstances, forcing Perrigo to focus instead on reducing its debt load and managing its capital much more efficiently, with the aim of improving profits.

This has uncertain implications for market sentiment, as Perrigo needs to improve its profitability and may need to invest. If the company is not granted a loan, a share capital increase on the stock market is out of the question. It would then have to resort to extra divestitures, which, in our opinion, cannot be ruled out at this point. In this case, however, it would be necessary to downsize the portfolio before the conditions for growth could be created. But everything takes time, and, from a strategic perspective, also accepting the risk of missing out on a targeted economic cycle.

Even More Challenges

In addition, there are several challenges ahead. These include:

1) An uncertain outcome to a class action lawsuit by investors stemming from unexpected operational issues in its infant formula business.

2) Sales of infant formula, which may risk losing money along the way as it clashes with the demographics of an aging population.

3) A slow recovery in profitability, which could be a concern.

4) While not core to the business, the company could consider selling non-core businesses. In a worst-case scenario, the company could be forced to consider selling non-prescription products for age-related conditions to strengthen its balance sheet and reduce its overall debt. For example: ointments, creams, cosmetics, vitamins, dietary supplements, etc. These sales figures reflect the tendency for people to hide the signs of aging in an ever-aging population. Because childhood, which does not get a boost if the birth rate is in crisis, is only a stage in the development towards an increasingly older age: we start aging as soon as we are born. Population aging is the result of a falling birth rate and an increase in life expectancy thanks to technological and scientific advances, especially in medicine. This is especially true in the most developed economies.

Conclusion

A hold rating currently advises caution, and, given PRGO's strong institutional investor presence, it could be a reasonable assessment of the stock.

Perrigo Company plc pays a secure and even increased dividend, but ongoing uncertainty is weighing on market sentiment. The stock has lost significant value in recent years and has been downgraded from mid-cap to small-cap. It is questionable whether investor sentiment can withstand further uncertainty.

The company needs to demonstrate that it can increase profitability as the market expects. While it is reducing its debt, it is also divesting. The purchase of infant formula seems unconvincing, and the market was caught off guard when Perrigo had to announce unexpected costs and a downwardly revised profitability forecast. Improving the situation will take considerable time and effort, and the Fed's interest rate cut may not be sufficient.

data.title: A Decade Of Dividends: Why Ares Capital Is Still Our +9% Yielding Anchor In 2026
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving.

It's hard to believe that High Dividend Opportunities has been publishing on Seeking Alpha for 10 years now. A lot has changed over the years on the platform and within our service.

One thing that hasn't changed is our focus on finding the best dividend-paying opportunities. Over the years, we've had some great investments, and we've had some that didn't work out. That's just a reality of investing.

Over the years, we've created a cohesive set of rules and guidelines that we call The Income Method. It's a strategy that seeks to generate a significant amount of cash flow for your portfolio and grow that income stream year after year.

Today, we are going to take a look at a holding that was first added to our portfolio in September 2016. This holding exemplifies the features we want in the companies that we invest in.

No, this stock hasn't rocketed to the moon, and it never will. It isn't a company that's going to be part of a big bubble, and it will never be part of the cool stock club on Reddit. It is a pleasantly boring company that generates cash flow every quarter.

Its total return is generated one dividend at a time. Not from rocketing up in price. A slow and steady grind to very strong and predictable returns.

Ares Capital—Yield 9.6%

Ares Capital (ARCC) is the largest publicly traded BDC (Business Development Company) with a portfolio fair value of ~$29.5 billion as of December 31, 2025. ARCC reported a pleasantly boring quarter. NII (Net Investment Income) came in at $0.52/share, up from $0.48 last quarter and down from $0.55 in Q4 of last year: Source.

NAV (Net Asset Value) was $19.94, down slightly from Q3 but up slightly year-over-year. Like all BDCs, ARCC has seen the average yield on its portfolio decline because interest rates are lower and ARCC's assets are primarily floating-rate.

ARCC came into 2025 with very low leverage, which means that they were able to leverage up as the year went on to offset the headwinds of declining interest rates.

Credit quality remains consistent, with non-accruals at 1.8% of amortized cost and 1.2% of fair value:

It is another "boring" quarter in the best way possible. ARCC is lending capital, minimizing credit losses, and passing along the profits to us as dividends.

A Look at ARCC's History

ARCC is the longest-held holding in the Model Portfolio. We first bought it back in September 2016; it was out of the portfolio for a month (May to June) in 2017 and has otherwise been held ever since.

Since we added it in September 2016 for $15.54/share, ARCC has paid $15.94 in dividends. The dividend that ARCC is paying today is 26% higher than it was when we bought it. And ARCC's price is currently about 24% higher.

By every measure, ARCC has been a successful investment that has achieved what we wanted when we bought it. We bought it primarily because we wanted to collect $0.38/quarter in dividends. We are currently collecting $0.48/quarter.

But it wasn't always a smooth ride. At one point, ARCC's price was down nearly 50%. And you know what? We're going to tell you right now that it's probably going to dip +50% again. We don't know when, we don't know what will be happening in the world, but someday, whether it is next year or 10 years from now, ARCC will probably trade below $10 again.

That's what markets do. The few dollars in unrealized capital gains we have will disappear. The average line is approximately where we initially bought:

And then they will come back. When the price falls from $19 down to $9, like it did in 2020, that feels very significant in the moment. Especially relative to the $0.40 dividend it was paying. Next to a single $0.40 dividend, a $10 price drop is huge.

But it isn't one dividend. We've collected 36 regular dividends and 8 supplemental dividends for a total of $15.94 and counting. Next to that, the $3-$4 in capital gains is nice, but not particularly relevant.

Total Return

People love to talk about "total return", where they compare stocks side-by-side using a measure that incorporates dividends. This is certainly important for a stock like ARCC, where substantially all of the return will come as dividends. If you are solely looking at price, you are ignoring the $15.94/share we collected in dividends over the past 10 years.

So, we can look at a chart like this one, which will estimate the value of dividend reinvestment from 9/20/2016:

Great, right?

Two problems:

  1. It measures a total return that is only relevant if you are selling today. Are you really exiting the stock market? Probably not. Even if you sold ARCC today, you're probably investing that money into a different stock.
  2. It assumes that you're reinvesting 100% of the dividends. Many aren't. One of the greatest benefits of dividends is that you have cash flow that you can use for whatever you want.

These two things are often not true. As a result, investors are making investment decisions based upon assumptions that aren't and will never be true.

The Flexibility of Dividends

The wonderful thing about dividends is that they can be used for anything. Yes, reinvesting in the same company that paid them is an option, but it often isn't the best option. ARCC paid $0.40/share on March 31st, 2020, when it was trading for $10.78/share, clearly a bargain opportunity to reinvest immediately. When ARCC was trading above $23 in February 2025, we wrote a note to members saying:

"ARCC is an anchor that is a great addition to any portfolio. You can expect ARCC to continue to provide ample income and remain a leader in the BDC space. Recently, ARCC's valuation has gotten a bit rich, so when looking to add more I would wait for the valuation to come down."

There was probably another stock in your portfolio that was trading at a cheaper valuation. Taking 30 seconds of your quarter to think about whether the company that paid the dividend is the best place to reinvest is time well spent. Just because a company is one that you like and want to hold long-term does not mean that it is trading at a great price to buy every single day.

Also, a major reason that investors choose an income investment strategy is that they need/want to withdraw cash from their portfolios. Much of the investing world focuses on investment returns that assume you never withdraw money. Every time you look at the "total return" of an index fund like the State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), you are looking at a calculation that assumes you never withdrew a penny.

What's the point of investing if you are never going to use the money you make? That's like going to work and never picking up your paycheck! We are doing this investment exercise for a reason, and that reason is that you want to take out the money someday, and maybe even today.

This is why we like to use a tool called Portfolio Visualizer, which allows you to simulate the impact of withdrawing money. Here is ARCC with withdrawals adjusted for inflation. We started the withdrawals at $65/month on a $10k investment. Note that "Income" is the dividends collected, and "Cash Flow" is the amount of cash withdrawn for each year. Any income that isn't withdrawn is reinvested: Source.

Ten years later, this $10k investment in ARCC is producing over $1,500/year in dividends, and our inflation-adjusted withdrawal requirement in 2025 was $1,041, or 68% of dividends collected.

In total, $8,406.25 was withdrawn from the portfolio out of a $10,000 investment without a single share being sold. The remaining shares are producing over $1,500/year in dividends, and if you wanted to sell them, the position is worth $5,830 more than you originally invested.

But why would you sell an investment that is achieving all of your goals?

Conclusion

Investors often get overly emotional over short-term price swings. Over short periods, a $5 swing in price is huge relative to the dividend, but when you buy a great company like ARCC and just let those dividends accumulate, the price swings become an increasingly small portion of your total return.

It isn't that we believe total returns are irrelevant; it's that, with time, the impact that the price has on your total return becomes increasingly irrelevant because you realize small gains with every dividend paid, and those small gains add up over long periods of time.

ARCC is a shining example of the type of companies we attempt to find. It produces a substantial amount of dividends with a business model that is relatively stable and predictable. ARCC is the largest publicly traded BDC and uses its size to invest in larger companies while remaining highly diversified. Avoiding the risk that smaller peers have, where a handful of defaults can have a lasting impact on book value and cash flow.

High yield, reliability, and consistency are all traits that ARCC has. That's why ARCC has become a cornerstone of our dividend portfolio. When the price pulls back and is trading below NAV, like it is today, it's a great time to start a position or add to an existing one.

data.title: Heartland Opportunistic Value Equity Strategy Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fourth Quarter Market Discussion

While AI dominated the 2025 equity market narrative, many perceived beneficiaries experienced a late fourth-quarter pullback. Despite this late volatility, overall market breadth narrowed as mega-cap stocks significantly outperformed during the first six weeks of the quarter. The Russell 2000® Index of small stocks returned 2.19% in the final three months of the year, trailing the 2.66% gain for the S&P 500.

Reflecting on 2025, we are pleased with the Strategy’s absolute performance. While we never want to underperform, we’re not surprised that keeping up with broad indices has proven challenging. Speculative investors have confidently rewarded perceived AI winners with unsustainably high valuations. Only time will tell whether fundamentals will remain strong enough for long enough to justify the excessive valuation assigned to pockets of the equity market.

Despite what appeared to be a one-way-train equity market in 2025, it’s worth noting that volatility in shares of seeming winners serves as a reminder that nobody knows which companies will be long-term winners from the proliferation of AI.

For instance, we previously owned a holding that was widely viewed as an AI leader owing to the company’s strategy to be an arms dealer of datacenter capacity. We appeared foolish for eliminating the position in August when shares rose over 40% from our exit price following the company’s second quarter earnings report in September. We sold the company because capital spending skyrocketed to a point where it was no longer generating free cash flow, a first in over thirty years. The market added more than $300 billion to its market capitalization because of a large order from OpenAI. By early December, more than $500 billion was erased from the company’s capitalization as the market began questioning how OpenAI would fund the order. By OpenAI’s own admission, it will not be profitable until 2028 at the earliest and analysts suggest 2030 may even be optimistic. To be clear, our thesis when first purchasing this company in 2016 had nothing to do with datacenters or AI. Rather, we saw an opportunity to buy an entrenched database business transitioning to a software-as-a-service business model at an attractive valuation of free cash flow and earnings.

On the other side of sentiment, Alphabet’s Google search engine was seen as at risk of disruption by OpenAI and other AI startups. In May of 2025, shares of Alphabet were trading at a 30% discount to the S&P 500, a bargain in our opinion. Not because we ‘knew’ that Alphabet was the AI winner. Rather, we viewed Alphabet as having key assets required to be a meaningful player in the AI race (consistent free cash flow, multiple 5+ billion user apps, real-time data, cloud computing infrastructure, custom application-specific semiconductors, and its own large-language model). Most importantly, the discounted valuation provided us with a margin of safety that gave us comfort in knowing that we didn’t need to be perfectly right in our assessment of the business to make money. Since May, shares of Alphabet have more than doubled.

Why is it that companies with market capitalizations measured in the hundreds of billions or multi trillions are moving with such extreme volatility? Is the market not efficient? By our count, both companies have more than thirty smart Wall Street analysts covering the companies. For this reason, it’s hard to believe the market isn’t efficient. We believe the answer lies in how little market participants ‘know’ about the future, which is inherently uncertain. When it comes to revolutionary technology, the future is more uncertain than normal. As a result, participants will act like they ‘know’ by bidding up shares of whatever company looks like the winner right now. We have not and will not play this game. Rather, we will be realistic about what is knowable, what we don’t know, and, critically, what the market is pricing in when allocating capital.

The market’s extreme reactions, and the speed with which they are taking place, illustrate how fleeting investor confidence can be. At Heartland, considering a range of possible outcomes is core to our process. Before initiating a position in any stock, we play out a variety of scenarios, reflecting how the market might value each business under different conditions. This includes a base case, a ‘blue sky’ scenario, a ‘downside’ scenario should circumstances worsen, and a ‘max downside’ case in the event of a potential shock.

Under any circumstance, we believe this approach is important for determining the realistic risk/reward proposition for securities under consideration. But it will be especially useful as the market broadens, a process that paused in the fourth quarter. For instance, while investors have been willing to look beyond mega-cap tech lately, non-earners among small-caps continue to outperform, a sign that speculation hasn’t gone away. Even within the value universe, junkier parts of the market continue to lead, even though high quality value has outperformed over the long run (see the charts below).

Source: The Leuthold Group. Monthly data from 12/31/1996 to 10/31/2025. The data in this chart shows the comparison of Quality Performance to Quality/Junky Value over time. All performance and return numbers are based on monthly rebalancing and members are equal-weighted. Value is a composite that consists of P/E, P/Sales, P/book and this factor is ranked across the largest 1500 stock universe and the cheapest quintile is considered Value. Quality is defined by 3 factors: ROE, Leverage (debt/assets) and operational (sales and earnings) stability. Then, ranked the largest 1500 US stocks by this composite 3-factor quality rank. The top quintile is high quality and bottom quintile low quality. Quality Value is defined as the cheapest stocks that are also in the top 2 quintiles of the quality rank. Junky Value is defined as the cheapest quintile stocks that are also in the bottom 3 quintiles of the quality rank. All indices are unmanaged. It is not possible to invest directly in an index. Past performance does not guarantee future results.

Value is a composite that consists of P/E, P/Sales, P/book and this factor is ranked across the largest 1500 stock universe and the cheapest quintile is considered Value. Past performance does not guarantee future results.

As the market breadth improves, considering a variety of outcomes should help investors weigh the full range of risks. At Heartland, another way we try to control for risk is by focusing on our 10 Principles of Value Investing™, which directs us to attractively priced, financially sound, and well-managed businesses that can grow intrinsic value.

Attribution Analysis

During the quarter, our Strategy returned 0.79%, trailing the Russell 3000® Value Index, which was up 3.78%. Stock selection was mixed with 8 of the 11 underperforming, most notably Information Technology and Healthcare.

In absolute terms, the smallest stocks in our portfolio, representing companies with market caps below roughly $750 million, were our Strategy’s best performers. However, when it comes to the impact of security selection, we had the most success with mega-cap and mid-cap names, while the selection effect among small-cap was fairly neutral. Part of this goes back to the fact that many top returners in the small-cap benchmark were profitless companies, a group we generally avoid due to our adherence to the 10 Principles of Value Investing™.

Portfolio Activity

One profitable company whose shares we’ve held for years, and that demonstrates why a focus on shareholder value creation matters in the long run, is Old Republic International (ORI), a specialty property & casualty commercial insurance underwriter.

Old Republic is a classic self-help story. We initiated the position in 2019 and added to it in 2020, as the shares took a hit due to the start of the pandemic. Shortly after, ORI was transformed from an insular insurance underwriter with disparate assets to a well-run company with a management team that uses capital allocation as a lever for value creation. That was after current CEO Craig Smiddy took over the company. Prior management made a bad decision in the early 2000s by entering the mortgage insurance market.

Smiddy unwound the mortgage insurance portfolio, extracted excess capital, and returned that capital to shareholders. He and his team have proven to be admirable capital allocators. When the stock was highly discounted relative to intrinsic value, from 2022 to 2024, 20% of the company was repurchased. When the shares were more fairly valued, management authorized five special dividends over our ownership period. Cumulatively, all the dividends that ORI has issued through this time, including ordinary quarterly payouts, have totaled more than $15 a share, which is remarkable for a stock that was trading in the low-to-mid $20 range in 2019.

Looking ahead, the company should continue to grow organically by investing in new short-tail lines of business by recruiting best-in-class talent to add niche exposures with sustainable and high return potential. Managers who join ORI buy into the business over a 10-year period with ultimate payout not realized for 15 years.

The stock currently trades at roughly fair value on an absolute basis while still being at a discount relative to its larger peers. We expect the CEO to continue driving intrinsic value growth in the coming years.

A new mid-cap holding in the quarter was Prestige Consumer Healthcare (PBH), whose over-the-counter brands are familiar to many including Dramamine motion sickness relief, Clear Eyes eye drops, and CompoundW wart removal products among others. The stock had been a laggard, losing more than 30% since mid-May, thanks to supply chain issues related to its eye care business. PBH’s supplier was simply not able to ship enough products to meet demand for the company’s Clear Eyes business, causing Prestige to lose sales and lower 2025 profit guidance.

The company has since fixed this issue by acquiring that supplier for $100 million. This asset acquisition raised concerns that management was pivoting their capital allocation strategy because they had historically outsourced manufacturing to third parties. We believe the manufacturing asset purchase is a function of tight ophthalmic industry production capacity and the high regulatory hurdles that must be met to add more capacity.

The eye care business should recover as customers re-stock, and we expect the company’s 2027 earnings to accelerate now that its supply chain issues are behind it. Yet after the sell-off, PBH shares trade at less than 13 times earnings, based on consensus estimates for profits over the next 12 months. Meanwhile, the stock sports a free cash flow-to-enterprise value yield of nearly 6.5%.

A multi-year holding that has struggled recently is Watsco (WSO), the largest wholesale distributor of HVAC and refrigeration equipment in the United States. The company is run by father-son duo Albert (AL) and AJ Nahmad. In 1973, Al acquired a controlling stake in the company when it was a small components manufacturer. He soon pivoted the company’s strategy to distribution, and he has built a holding company that focuses on capital allocation and on building technology infrastructure that can be leveraged by their independently run business units and by the company’s HVAC contractor customers. A demand lull caused by declining new home construction activity, the weakening financial health of consumers, and the fact that demand had been pulled forward the prior year ahead of known price increases drove the stock down more than a third since February.

Why are we still attracted to this business? For starters, around 85% of its revenues are tied to the replacement of HVAC systems and components, where demand is driven by equipment failure, regardless of where we are in the economic cycle. In addition, HVAC original equipment manufacturing is a highly consolidated industry, and government efficiency standards continue to add costs to the hardware that WSO sells. This creates a backdrop where Watsco benefits from inflation in products sold. Meanwhile, its capital-light business model, where capex accounts for only around 0.5% of sales, helps to ensure that inflation won’t chip away at cash flow available for shareholder returns or growth investments.

While the market seems to be focused on short-term volume headwinds, WSO is positioned to benefit from technology investments that uniquely favor it. For example, the company has a modern tech stack and a fully digitized platform, unlike most of its peers. In recent years, management has deployed pricing optimization tools that price products based on data unique to each customer.

The company is also using AI to identify pricing outliers that can be addressed by their sales team quickly and has made investments in AI-enhanced customer service, technical support, and software development, which we believe will drive margin expansion and share gains. Meanwhile, the stock sports a free cash flow-to-enterprise value yield of around 5%, which is closer to 7% on a normalized basis. We used recent weakness in shares to increase our position in the company.

Outlook

We continue to view this market opportunistically and we are well positioned for a sustained broadening of the equity market. Yet recent volatility surrounding the AI trade, and the speed with which investors have been reassessing the potential winners and losers of this theme, reinforces the importance of considering a range of potential outcomes. Our willingness to consider these ‘what ifs?’ and our 10 Principles of Value Investing™ will continue to drive our process as a new wave of opportunities — and risks — present themselves.

Thank you for your continued trust and confidence.

data.title: Zebra Technologies Q4 Earnings: Beat Signals A Powerful AI Pivot
data.description:
Reviewing Zebra Technologies' Q4 Results

I covered Zebra Technologies Corporation (ZBRA) only once, in June 2023, and the stock hasn't moved in the direction I expected. It did rise as I anticipated, gaining almost 50% at one point, but then lost momentum and sold off again, losing about one-third of its value since the beginning of 2025. Yesterday, however, we saw ZBRA's attempt to recover, supported by its stronger-than-anticipated Q4 results. Unfortunately for long-term holders and those buying the rip, ZBRA couldn't settle above its 200-day moving average and the medium-term downtrend line.

Let's take a look at the fundamentals that came out yesterday to get a better understanding of what's going on under the hood, and what the odds are for the current "recovery attempt" to continue.

Zebra reported $1.48 billion in revenues (+10.57% YoY) and $4.33 in adjusted EPS (+8.25% YoY) for Q4, beating on the top line by 0.6%, but reporting just in line with the consensus estimate on the bottom line. Despite reporting in line with the consensus, the firm actually beat its own guidance on the EPS level (vs. the high-end figure ). Also, the management issued bullish FY2026 guidance on the call, projecting 9-13% sales growth and non-GAAP EPS of $17.70-18.30 for the year, implying a YoY growth of ~13.63% (at the midpoint).

But the analysts who cover ZBRA decided to downgrade their earnings estimates for the upcoming quarter, based on Seeking Alpha's data:

Starting in Q4 2025, Zebra now reports through Connected Frontline (CF) and Asset Visibility & Automation (AVA) business segments after the decision to exit the autonomous mobile robotics business and focus mainly on higher-margin RFID (Radio Frequency Identification), machine vision, and the new Frontline AI Suite (also higher-margin compared to hardware assets).

In the past, Zebra used to be quite sensitive to destocking cycles and inventory gluts. But now, thanks to AI tech it has acquired and developed in-house (plus partnerships with large tech giants), it's shifting into a "central nervous system" that can provide predictive analytics rather than just simple data capture.

We've added RFID to all our next-generation mobile devices. We're increasing memory and processing speeds, working with our partners in Qualcomm and certainly Google on the OS to make sure that they can support AI models on the device. So they're seeing value in things like mobile computing. We're doing the same across the entire portfolio, adding AI capabilities, capabilities to machine vision, continuing to enhance capabilities around scanning, our print portfolio, we're adding RFID printing to that portfolio.

Source: Zebra's earnings call

They acquired Photoneo and Matrox Imaging, and these purchases now help Zebra go beyond simple barcode readers. For example, the FS42 fixed industrial scanner uses an NPU (Neural Processing Unit) tech for deep learning inspections that identify anomalies and defects on production lines in real time. AI models interpret the physical world and feed that data into predictive systems, and it adds a massive value layer on Zebra's selling products/offerings, in my opinion. These predictive insights go to the Frontline AI Suite, which is basically an interface that then takes these insights and hands them to the workforce through three critical pillars: Enablers, Blueprints, and the Zebra Companion. The Zebra Companion agent allows staff to ask questions about standard operating procedures/tasks, and the "Blueprints" automate multi-step tasks, streamlining the whole process. I like the direction of where Zebra puts its money, trying to develop a better stack that can potentially increase their margins in the long-term. Also, I don't think their offering is too sensitive to all the SaaS-related issues that common CRM providers are going through after the emergence of AI agents - Zebra has managed to build a moat that is hard to replace with a simple tool, or with an ordinary AI-driven workflow.

In 2026, Zebra is going to hike prices and shift the product design to higher-density memory to offset the 2-point gross margin headwind from memory pricing, and if they do it efficiently (not losing volumes due to pricing hikes), I expect to see some upgrades to the guidance by 2H 2026.

We are currently facing industry-wide price increases for memory components beginning in Q2. Our full year guide reflects us fully mitigating this approximately 2-point headwind and driving profitable growth in 2026 through multiple initiatives, including collaborating closely with our vendors to manage supply, targeted price increases, net savings from the robotics business exit, targeted actions to drive productivity, as well as FX favorability.

Source: Zebra's earnings call

Analysts seem to be cautious on EPS figures beyond Q1 2026, so Zebra needs to show a clean gross margin print in Q2 to make the bullish repricing happen (or the management should sound positive on Q2 margins stability at the next earnings call).

I like the fact that Zebra keeps investing in acquisitions, which happen to be accretive as we've seen in Q4 - without them, the organic growth is just 2.5%:

In FY2026, management expects to generate ~$900 million in free cash flow, so I expect more targets to be acquired to boost Zebra's effectiveness. On top of that, it's important to note that ZBRA spent ~$587 million on buybacks in FY2025, and the Board authorized another $1 billion in February 2026. With 50% of FCF flowing to buybacks, that should support an eventual repricing to the upside, as valuation multiples usually follow this supply-cut pattern quite well.

Now, let's talk about ZBRA's valuation setup, which looks quite favorable for buyers, in my opinion.

Zebar used to trade at 25x+ for a long time, but as I'm writing these lines, the market gives ZBRA only 15.5x in forward P/E:

At the very same time, the analysts who form the EPS consensus expect to see Zebra's EPS almost doubling over the next 5 years:

At this projected EPS growth level, I think ZBRA has become too cheap, especially as we see its business momentum turning up thanks to AI optimizations and solidly executed M&As.

I think Zebra should trade at a P/E of 18-20x, slightly below its historical multiple (just to play safe here). Assuming no premium on the FY2027 EPS consensus of $20.02, I get a price target of $380.4/share. That's 38-39% above the current stock price over the next 12-24 months.

Risks

I'm a bit concerned about the global memory chip shortage and the impact it could have on Zebra. Management said there should be a 2-point gross margin headwind in Q2, which is why analysts lowered their projections for that period. However, my thesis assumes that Zebra will be able to mitigate this through price increases and supply chain shifts - that's where the repricing and valuation expansion might come from, moving ZBRA closer to my price target. In reality, that might not happen. If ZBRA's P/E stays in the range of 12-14x by the end of 2027, and analysts cut the current FY2027 EPS consensus by 10%, my price target would drop to $234.23 per share, implying a downside potential of 14-15% from today's levels.

The Bottom Line

Despite the risks, I like Zebra's continued development toward AI integration and management's focus on accretive acquisitions that drive value. The stock's flat performance since my 2023 call has been mainly due to multiple contractions - the business was actually doing okay. At least there were no abrupt corrections in sales or EBITDA. Yes, there was some stagnation, but now it looks like it's going to fade shortly. I see an upside potential of 38-39% over the next 1-2 years, so I remain bullish on Zebar after its Q4 print.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: FedEx Investor Day: Strong Run, Weak Catalyst (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
FedEx Corporation (FDX) hosted its Investor Day on the 12th of February, outlining its growth targets for 2029, consisting of revenue opportunities and cost reduction to drive improvements in operating income. In this report, I discuss the revenue and cost initiatives and update my price target. FedEx stock has surged 60% since and exceeded the 53.6% upside I saw for the company based on earnings for 2027 outlined in my prior report, so I would say that the growth trajectory in the years ahead will be pivotal to the company’s ability to drive additional upside to the stock price.

FedEx Aims For $98 Billion In Sales And $6 Billion In Free Cash Flow

For 2029, FedEx is aiming to generate $98 billion in sales, $8 billion in operating income at 8% margins with $6 billion in free cash flow, and $25 in GAAP earnings per share. These figures exclude FedEx Freight. As a reference, FedEx’s latest full-year revenues, including FedEx Freight, were $87.9 billion with EBIT of $6.1 billion.

Revenue Growth Driver Focuses On B2B

From 2026 to 2029, FedEx revenues excluding FedEx Freight are expected to increase from $85 billion to $98 billion. This is a total increase of around $13-$14 billion, with $6 billion driven by volume, $6 billion driven by yield, and $1 billion driven by corporate & others. International and US domestic revenues are expected to grow 4% annually.

In the B2B market, FedEx sees growth opportunities in healthcare, automotive, data centers, and aerospace. The healthcare and pharma market is valued at $120 billion. FedEx generated over $9 billion in the healthcare market in 2025 with 9% revenue growth in the pharma market through Q2 2026. The automotive market is valued at $25 billion, with $4.4 billion in FY25 sales, while data centers are a largely untapped $7 billion opportunity. Aerospace is an $11 billion market with an estimated $1 billion in sales for FY26. The total addressable markets give some sense of growth opportunities. However, there are no set targets for 2029. What we do know is that it is high-yield markets that should largely drive the $6 billion in additional sales. The small business revenues have increased 13% year-on-year. The B2C market is valued at $95 billion, and FedEx targets low single-digit volume growth there.

So, putting that together gives revenue growth as a combination of yield from the B2B market and volume growth from the B2C market. However, in terms of detail, I found the revenue outlook lacking depth.

The Second Axis Of Earnings Growth

The other axis of growth is cost optimization through network, digital, and organizational transformations. This is organized in two main cost reduction programs. The first one is the Drive program, with $4 billion in cumulated cost savings in 2025, and Network 2.0 driving $2 billion in sales for FY27. The company processes about 2 petabytes of data each day, and that data fed into AI informs decision-making. The company is redesigning 9 sort processes into one unified mega process. Secondly, legacy systems will be retired to decrease run costs. In the network, the company has increased hub productivity by 20%, while in the US, costs have been brought back down for pickup and delivery costs by 10%. The acquisition of InPost fits in the strengthening of the last-mile delivery capability in Europe.

As part of the Network 2.0 initiative, eligible stations in the US and Canada are being optimized. To date, 25% has been optimized while 300 stations have been closed, driving 10% cost reductions. By the end of this calendar year, 65% should be optimized with over 475 stations closed.

The TriColor network provides FedEx with flexibility. The purple network is focused on high-value, high-margin, high-priority transport, while the orange network focuses on off-cycle operations and the white network focuses on utilizing 3rd-party logistics providers to meet demand. This flexible setup allowed Trans-Pacific Asia capacity to be adjusted down by 27%, with 25% reductions in the purple network and 35% in the white network. In the Asia-Europe lane, purple capacity grew 20% to capitalize on premium growth opportunities, while white network capacity was brought down by 30%.

FedEx Investor Day Was Not Inspiring

If we look at the income bridge, we see that the company expects $2 billion in income lift from volumes, $6 billion in yield partially offset by $5 billion in higher expenses driven by labor cost increases. I always appreciate the mid-to-long-term picture that is provided during Investor Days. However, what FedEx did present was not particularly inspiring. In terms of revenues, they did not provide a lot of details on revenues in each end market by 2029, and if we look at costs, we see that many of the cost improvements come from programs that have already been underway, and the company emphasized the already realized costs more than it actually detailed cost reductions by year or implemented additional cost measures that were not previously anticipated. Overall, it was a rather dull Investor Day presentation, as it didn’t contain a lot that couldn’t already be known, and some growth drivers were not detailed for the years ahead. The company provided color on what the company’s performance is expected to be without FedEx Freight, which will be spun off, but that was really about it.

FDX Stock Downgraded To Hold

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

I am downgrading FedEx stock from buy to hold. For FedEx as a whole, I anticipate 4.5% annual growth in sales, 5.3% annual growth in EBITDA, and 16.5% growth in free cash flow. We do note that expectations have come up a bit for the 2025-2027 period, but not enough to continue marking the stock a buy, as the value gap has essentially closed, leaving no upside for stock price when looking at the earnings one year ahead.

Margins are expected to dip this year as FedEx will have some costs associated with its efficiency initiatives, and from there margins will recover. I believe that overall, the margins do show resilience in the sense that FedEx seems to be able to continue to find levers to regrow margins even when the market conditions are not favorable. In terms of cash conversion, we note that there are improvements in the years ahead, with cash conversion expected to hit 39% by 2028. This marks a 10-point increase compared to 2025. Capital intensity is also set to decrease. I have currently modelled in the decline in the shareholder returns as the increase in the cash position essentially comes from FedEx Freight issuing $3.7 billion in notes for the spin-off. By 2028, there should be around $8 billion in headspace for shareholder repurchases if FedEx targets a 3x net debt leverage, but it is not something that is going to drive fundamental upside from the current trading levels.

Conclusion: FedEx Had A Good Run, But The Investor Day Was Not Inspiring

FedEx’s Investor Day presentation, in my view, was somewhat underwhelming. The company did outline targets for FedEx without FedEx Freight, but many of the growth drivers the company cited were mostly backward-looking rather than providing detail on the forward expectations. The company does expect 4% annual sales growth through 2029 and substantial margin improvements. However, I feel like the company has been selective and vague on the exact growth drivers. That is not a reason to downgrade the stock. I am downgrading the stock for the simple reason that the upside anticipated has materialized, and after a 60% increase in the stock price, it might be a good time to take profit. The stock is not a sell, but some profit-taking would be healthy, and for a buy rating, we need substantially stronger volume growth and yield expansion.

data.title: Buy 5 IDEAL S&P 600 Small/MidCap 'Safer' February DiviDogs
data.description:
Foreword

What Is the S&P 600?

“The S&P 600 is an index of small-cap stocks managed by Standard & Poor's. It tracks a broad range of small-sized companies that meet specific liquidity and stability requirements. This is determined by specific metrics such as public float, market capitalization, and financial viability, among other factors.

KEY TAKEAWAYS

  • The S&P 600 is a benchmark index for small-cap stocks published by Standard & Poor's.
  • To be listed on the S&P 600, stocks must have a market cap of $850 million to $3.6 billion, preventing overlap with S&P's larger cap indices.
  • Several index ETFs and mutual funds allow investors to track the performance of the S&P 600 small-cap index.
  • The main reason to invest in small caps is that they have more room to grow than large caps.
  • Small-cap stocks are not as closely followed by professional analysts, allowing investors to “get in” on these companies while they’re flying under Wall Street’s radar.

Understanding the S&P 600

The S&P SmallCap 600 Index was established by Standard & Poor's (the creator of the S&P 500). It uses a capitalization-weighted index to broadly track the performance of small-cap stocks on the U.S. equities market. It includes 600 companies and represents close to 3% of the U.S. market.

Unlike many other small-cap benchmarks, the S&P 600 has an earnings requirement, which is used to ensure the quality of the stocks included and hedge against volatility. To be included, a company must have a market capitalization between $1 billion and $6.7 billion.

It must also:

  • Be a U.S. company
  • Maintain at least 10% of its shares outstanding
  • Have positive earnings for both its most recent quarter and the sum of its trailing four consecutive quarters.”—Investopedia.com

While 2 in this collection of 56 S&P600 small/mid-cap dividend stocks are too pricey and reveal only skinny dividends, 10 show negative safety margins and 37 others show negative returns, leaving 5 of the of the 56 highest yield ‘safer’ S&P600 Dogs ready to buy. February finds:

Adamas Trust Inc (ADAM); Global Net Lease (GNL); Universal Health Realty Income Trust (UHT); Navient Corp (NAVI); LTC Properties Inc (LTC), living up to the dogcatcher ideal of annual dividends from $1K invested exceeding single share prices and they are also ‘safer’ because they have free cash flow adequate to support their dividends.

[See a summary of top ten fair-priced S&P600 small/mid-cap Dogs in Actionable Conclusion 21 near the mid-point of this article.]

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Expect 31.0% To 61.07% Net Gains From Top-Ten S&P600 Small/MidCap Dogs By February 2026

Five of ten top dividend-yielding S&P500 divi-dogs (tinted gray in the chart below) were among the top ten price gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. So, this December, 2026 yield-based forecast for S&P600 small/mid-cap dogs, as graded by Wall St. wizard estimates, was 50% accurate.

Estimated dividend-returns from a $1,000.00 investment in each of the ten highest-yielding S&P600 stocks and their aggregate one-year analyst median target prices, as reported by YCharts, created the projections below. (Note: one-year target-prices estimated by lone analysts were not applied.) Ten probable profit-generating trades projected to February, 2027 were:

Shutterstock Inc (SSTK) was projected to net $610.73, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 3 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 26% greater than the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

data.title: NewLake Capital Partners: An Undervalued REIT With Double-Digit Yield
data.description:
Investment Thesis

NewLake Capital Partners (NLCP) is one of my favorite REIT stocks. NLCP provides real estate for cannabis companies for cultivation through long-term triple leases (10-20 years). NewLake Capital Partners is ranked number 1 among 13 REIT companies by Seeking Alpha Quant Ranking. It is a high-yield REIT stock with an approximate 11% yield. President Donald Trump has recently signed an executive order that will help to increase the research on cannabis and cannabidiol (CBD). This will reclassify marijuana to a Schedule-3 drug from a Schedule-1 drug. This will help in the research on the effect of cannabis as a painkiller and antidepressant on the human body. Though recreational use is still prohibited and comes under the same category as other illegal drugs. ​In my previous article I was bullish on NLCP and I still keep my “Strong Buy” rating.

Third Quarter Performance

Newlake’s revenue remained flat during the third quarter of FY25. The company announced a revenue of $12.6 million, reflecting no change on a YoY basis. Net income of the company slightly improved to $6.7 million in 3Q25 as compared to $6.3 million in 3Q24. NLCP’s surge in revenue is based on the new property acquisitions. On the contrary, a decline in the revenue reflects that a tenant defaulted. Since the revenue remained flat, which shows no acquisitions done by the company, but also no existing tenants defaulted either, which is a good sign.

Funds From Operations (FFO) were $10.7 million in the third quarter of FY25, while Adjusted Funds From Operations (AFFO) were $11.0 million in 3Q25.

Stable AFFO means that the rents were collected on time, no tenant defaulted, no tenant had material delinquencies, and dividends were paid on time by the company to its shareholders. Stability in cannabis REITs is rare. Stability in cannabis REITs is itself a positive sign, and that's why I am bullish on NLCP stock.

The demand for cannabis in the US market peaked in the past 30-35 years, especially since 2015. A study by Wiley Library – Society for the Study of Addiction, shows that from 1992 to 2022, the number of people consuming marijuana on a daily or near daily (DND) basis increased by 20x from 0.9 million to 17.17 million users. I believe this will play a key role in the flourishing of companies like NLCP, as the perception of US citizens has clearly shifted in the past 3 decades and especially the last decade. Data from 2023 by the U.S. Census Bureau shows that 88% American adults support the adult use of cannabis, while 54% American adults believe that alcohol is more harmful than cannabis. I think the statistical data shows a clear picture of the demand and once the legalities around cannabis are resolved, companies like NLCP will reap the benefit of it.

Another reason that makes me bullish on NLCP stock is that just 3% of its leases will expire in the next 6 years. 34% of its leases will expire in 2034 and 58% will expire thereafter. This means that more than half of its current leases will still be there even after 10 years and I am expecting that with current demand and perception of the general populace, I think the company will expand its cultivation assets and dispensaries and issue more leases.

Attractive Double-Digit Yield

​Another reason that makes me bullish on NLCP stock is that it has been consistently delivering value to shareholders with high yields. To me, this makes it an attractive choice for income-seeking investors looking for a high-quality dividend stock with a robust balance sheet. In 4Q21, the company was paying a dividend of $0.31, and in the last quarter, the company paid a dividend of $0.43. The company on 15 Dec, 2025 announced a dividend of $0.43 for the fourth quarter of FY25. The following data shows NLCP’s commitment to deliver value to its investors by consistently increasing its dividends over the years.

By comparing its dividend yield with its competitors in the industry, I can conclude that its yield is better than most of its top competitors in the industry. Only one stock, Innovative Industrial Properties, Inc. (IIPR) is outperforming, but I would still rate NLCP a better stock than IIPR. The reason I have is that NLCP has a very low debt of $7.6 million, and IIPR’s debt is $340.9 million. Also, revenue growth estimates of NLCP are extremely better than IIPR. 5 year CAGR of NLCP is 112%, and IIPR is 23%, making NLCP my top REIT stock.

What to Expect in 4Q25?

According to Seeking Alpha’s estimates, NLCP’s revenue is expected to remain flat. No significant changes are expected. If it is not increasing, it's not declining either, as it means that none of its tenants defaulted. This is in itself a good sign. Its tenants are paying rents on time, which keeps the earnings consistent in the 2nd to 4th quarter. It increases rent on a yearly basis, so an increase in revenue is expected in the first quarter of 2026. Though estimates are low for 1Q26, if vacant properties are leased to tenants, revenue will increase.

Its FFO is expected to be $0.51. Since the last 3 quarters, NLCP has beaten the estimates. So it's expected to continue the same trend.

NLCP did not provide any outlook, so it's obvious that the revenue will not witness a significant increase. In the long-run I expect more leases to be issued, addition of new cultivation assets, and dispensaries. This will increase their revenue and market share and eventually increase profits.

Valuation

Valuation ratios of NLCP also show a solid picture of undervalued REIT stock. NLCP is trading at a trailing P/E non-GAAP 12.19x (sector median 30.71x) and forward P/E non-GAAP 12x (sector median 31.26x). This shows that the stock is trading at an almost 60% discount as compared to its sector. A gap like this in most of the cases means that a business has a structural weakness or earnings instability. But looking at the NLCP’s income statement, I can say that its rental-based revenue provides stability. I think the consistency between its trailing and forward multiples shows its earnings are not going to decline, and the NLCP stock is undervalued.

The most important multiples to know the health of any REIT are AFFO and FFO. NLCP trades at trailing and forward P/AFFO and P/FFO of around 7.5x. This represents a discount of approximately 50% compared to the sector median. For REIT investors, AFFO shows the actual measure of distributable income. An AFFO multiple this low means that the company has inconsistent cash flows or reflects an elevated risk. Yet, NLCP covers its dividend payouts by AFFO. In my opinion, the market is underestimating its cash flow quality and mispricing risk. In my opinion, at current valuations, investors will be acquiring stable cash flows at a discounted price.

The strength of the balance sheet further supports my bullish thesis. Having less than 2% debt/equity ratio compared to 94% sector median, NLCP operates at a significantly lower leverage than its peers in the industry. Most of the companies are burdened with heavy interest rates, but NLCP is operating as an almost debt-free company, significantly reducing refinancing risk and increasing long-term stability. If a company has such characteristics, in my opinion, it should trade at a premium rather than a discount. ​

Technical Analysis - Price Forecast

My technical analysis of the NLCP stock supports my bullish thesis. As I marked in the chart, at an earlier point, you can see its all-time low, which is also its support and buyer interest level. At this point, the market achieved its liquidity (at around $11.84), and after this point, the price sidewaved and started consolidating, and because the buyer's order was filled, it gave an impulsive move. This indicates it was the buyer's interest level. After this, it rose and took daily swing liquidity. When it hit daily swing liquidity, it was meant to hit its previous support or bottom. And then it created a double bottom, which in itself is a strong bullish pattern. At this point, it created an order block with a fair value gap (FVG) and volume imbalance due to high buyer pressure. There is a significant chance that it will rise again from volume imbalance or order block and achieve the target.

In layman's terms, I would say that you can buy this stock in two or three steps. You can buy a minimum number of stocks at this point i.e $16. After this, you can buy around FVG, which is around $13.01, and finally, when it hits the order block, you can buy a maximum number of shares at a price of around $12.13. After this, the NLCP stock is supposed to hit the target, which is around $21.

So my price target for NLCP stock is $21, and that's why I rate this one a “Strong Buy”.

Risk

There is no risk-free business in the world. Companies like NewLake Capital Partners are also prone to risks, although they are not directly involved in manufacturing/producing and operate on the leasing of assets. One of the main risks that I have noticed is the tenants' indebtedness. Most of its tenants have a significant amount of debt and have expensive financing. This could lead to rent delays and could potentially result in the default of the tenants. In such circumstances, NLCP will also face a decline in revenue as most of its revenue comes from rents. Also, another risk associated with this is that most of its rent comes from a few tenants. So basically, they are NLCP’s large accounts. Problems with one tenant could potentially severely hurt NLCP’s earnings.

Regulatory risk is also looming over the cannabis businesses. As cannabis is federally illegal in the U.S., delays in regulation or unfavorable policies could result in the decline of tenants’ profitability.

REIT compliance risk is also a threat that cannot be underestimated. If the company fails to meet tax requirements, this could result in the loss of REIT status.

Conclusion

In my opinion, NLCP stock fundamentals are strong, the balance sheet is strong, and if you’re looking to expand your portfolio into REITs, NLCP could be the right choice. If you are an income-seeking investor, NLCP stock has a dividend yield of 11%. Once the cannabis market reaches maturity and regulatory restrictions ease, NLCP will reap the early mover advantage. There are risks as the regulations related to cannabis still pose a threat to the cannabis industry, but I believe in the long run, NLCP will be a good investment.

I keep my rating on NLCP stock a “Strong Buy”.
