data.title: PepsiCo: Solid Momentum Coming Into 2026, But I Still Rate It A Hold For Now
data.description:
Introduction

In 2025, I parted ways with my PepsiCo, Inc. (PEP) position. Since then, the stock has rallied due to a solid Q4 earnings report, giving the Dividend King momentum coming into the new year.

While I like their improvements, their dividend coverage is still something that concerns me. But management seems confident in the company's ability to sustain its dividend going forward. This is evidenced by the solid 4% dividend increase.

In this article, I discuss Pepsi's latest earnings, fundamentals, and why, despite their improvements, I continue to rate the stock a Hold for now.

Previous Hold Thesis

I last covered Pepsi back in October after their Q3 earnings. Despite the beat, declining operating and gross profit margins signaled headwinds still lingered.

Dividend safety was also a concern, as free cash flow failed to cover dividend payments during the quarter. However, strategic moves and cost-savings initiatives looked to position the company for future growth.

But I wanted to see sequential improvements before considering upgrading the stock. Since then, the share price has seen a strong rally, blowing past their price target of $153.38. PEP is up over 12% compared to the S&P (SP500), up a little over 5%.

Rally After Earnings

As mentioned earlier, Pepsi's share price rallied after earnings for a few reasons. But first, I want to touch on how the company did on earnings.

During Q4, the beverage giant posted a double beat. This was better than peer The Coca-Cola Company (KO), which missed revenue expectations by $250 million. This likely also contributed to the rally, as PEP has struggled in the past few quarters due to inflationary headwinds.

EPS amounted to $2.26, beating analysts' estimates by $0.02. Revenue of $29.43 billion beat by $370 million.

EPS climbed from $1.96 the previous year, while revenue grew a solid 5.6%. This was likely due to pricing power. But organic sales also contributed, up 2%. KO's organic revenue rose 5%.

EMEA saw growth of 5%. However, their North American segment offset this, down 1%. Food volumes also declined, down 2%. But beverage volumes managed to see a slight increase of 1%.

In my opinion, the decline in food volume likely has to do with inflationary impacts and the rapid adoption of GLP-1 drugs.

During the quarter, management touched on this, stating that they expect a broader adoption of GLP-1 drugs going forward. But acquisitions, product relaunches, and lower prices on products are expected to offset this somewhat.

Management stated that affordability is still an issue. But they have a strategy aimed at targeting lower- and middle-income consumers. This is the relaunch of products and lowering of prices. As a result, sales are expected to improve in the back half of the year.

2026 Outlook

PEP initiated their 2026 outlook, which also likely contributed to their price rally. The beverage giant expects 5% bottom line growth, which is solid for a company of their size. Organic revenue growth is expected to be slightly higher than 2025's 1.7%. For 2026, growth is projected between 2% and 4%.

Why I Reiterate My Hold Rating

Despite improvements, Pepsi still saw profits and margins decline. Operating profits fell 11% year-over-year from approximately $12.9 billion to $11.5 billion. As a result, margins declined from 14% to 12.2%.

On a positive note, the chart below shows margins are expected to increase in 2026 and beyond. However, this remains to be seen as macro environmental risks remain from inflationary impacts.

Earnings per share were also down slightly, $0.02 from $8.16. This was due to net income declining from $9.6 billion to $8.24 billion. As a Dividend King, seeing declining cash flows is troublesome.

On a positive note, management did raise the dividend 4%, from $5.69 to $5.92 on an annualized basis. While this does give me some confidence, this means the company needs to grow cash flows significantly to get the payout ratio to a comfortable level.

The $10 billion buyback program should increase dividend safety. This is expected to be completed by 2030. Buybacks will also likely lead to earnings growth.

And while buyback programs are great, this could also be an indicator of Pepsi's struggles to grow organically. Lower interest rates will likely provide tailwinds. But these will likely take another 6 to 12 months for consumers to feel the effects.

Meaning, there's a likelihood that PEP will continue to see headwinds, particularly in its North American segment. And as a result, margins will likely decline as well.

Dividend Safety

As mentioned previously, cash flows declined. Cash from operations declined year-over-year, as you can see below. But on a positive note, free cash flow increased due to lower capital expenditures.

PEP returned, as promised, $8.6 billion through buybacks and dividends. Shares outstanding were down from 1.378 billion to 1.373 billion. Using their 2025 annualized dividend of $5.69, PEP would need $7.81 billion in free cash flow to cover the dividend.

If you remove the share repurchases, PEP managed to cover the dividend. But this gives them little room for error. I prefer non-REIT companies to have a payout ratio at 60% or below.

This gives them a well-covered dividend. And it gives them room for error and acquisitions without having to increase leverage on the balance sheet too much. Speaking of balance sheets, Pepsi repaid $4.1 billion in debt.

Their balance sheet, consisting of an A credit rating, does give them flexibility. Long-term debt stood at $42.3 billion, with approximately $6.7 billion in short-term debt obligations.

Cash on hand was plentiful, with more than $9 billion in cash & cash equivalents. So, from a balance sheet perspective, PEP is in good shape. But an increase in leverage and tight dividend coverage are items to be cautious of going forward.

Growth Drivers

I touched on growth drivers for PEP going forward. In 2025, the company made strides with acquisitions like Siete & Poppi.

They also increased their stake in Celsius (CELH), and Elliott Management took a stake in PEP to make improvements. All positive signs, in my opinion.

Last month they also announced a key partnership with NVIDIA (NVDA) and Siemens to leverage AI to improve supply chain operations. So, PEP is doing all the right things to move the business forward.

Valuation

Because of their strong rally, I suspect any significant upside may be limited here. At the current price, Pepsi offers little upside to their price target. In the coming quarters, investors will likely be on edge to see if the beverage giant can continue to execute.

I agree with Wall Street, Quant, and other Seeking Alpha analysts that PEP is a hold. Improvements, dividend increase, and buyback announcement seem to be priced in here.

For investors who missed the rally when the stock was trading in the $130s and $140s, I would consider adding on a pullback in the $150s.

Using their expected EPS growth of 5%, this puts estimated earnings at $8.55 a share. This gives PEP a current forward P/E of 19.41x, below their 5-year average of 22.48x.

So, the stock appears slightly undervalued here. But as I mentioned previously, I don't see any significant upside until the company shows they can execute on expectations.

Risks & Takeaway

Despite improvements, inflation and tariff impacts will likely continue to weigh on profits and margins for the near term.

GLP-1 drugs also present risks, increasing the likelihood for food volumes to be suppressed.

At the current price, PEP's improvements are likely priced in, giving investors little upside in the near to medium term.

I would like to see how the company executes on buybacks as well as see improvements in their North American segment before considering upgrading them to a Buy.

data.title: 50% In Just 5 Stocks: Why I'm Willing To Invest Big In High-Quality
data.description:
Introduction

I have to start this article with a confession that may trigger a few people and make sense to others. As I believe many of you know already, I manage a highly concentrated portfolio. Last week, my portfolio became even more concentrated when I sold Home Depot (HD), which had become my smallest investment, to buy more QXO Inc. (QXO).

Now, I’m back to 15 individual stocks that account for roughly 70% of my total net worth (the rest is my real estate investment in Albania). However, that’s not everything. My five biggest holdings account for more than 50% of my portfolio, which means they hold close to 40% of my entire wealth.

These holdings are (in order):

  • LandBridge (LB)
  • Texas Pacific Land Corp. (TPL)
  • QXO Inc.
  • Old Dominion Freight Line (ODFL)
  • Carlisle Companies (CSL)

If you think that’s nuts, I’m glad to have your attention, as I am not writing this article to push these investments to you, but to explain to readers why I am willing to run such a concentrated strategy. I will tell you how I think about wealth generation and, even more important, how I pick stocks.

After all, ODFL, for example, has been featured in many recent articles. That’s not because I need to “pump” my own holdings, but because it is such a great example for countless articles, regardless of whether it’s about my cyclical growth thesis, valuation assessments, and examples of why dividend growth sometimes trumps dividend income.

In this article, I’ll also be able to explain why these companies make for such great examples. So, hopefully, at the end of this article, you better understand my reasoning and hopefully have learned something that could help you in your own investment process.

And it won’t hurt if I explain why I like my top 5 holdings so much.

So, as we have a lot to discuss, let’s get right to it!

Why My Strategy Is So Unique

Let’s start this part by giving you some facts. The S&P 500 holds 500 unique companies. However, its ten biggest holdings account for roughly 40% of its weighting (see below), which is one of the highest numbers in its history, thanks to the Big Tech dominance.

Meanwhile, the German DAX holds 40 individual companies. Its biggest five stocks account for roughly 43%, as you can see below.

In other words, although I’m giving you just two examples here, concentration, in general, often occurs, especially in smaller countries than the U.S., where a few very successful companies often dominate the main index.

However, I am way more concentrated than this, especially if we consider that LandBridge and Texas Pacific are both landowners in the Permian Basin, which means I have roughly 25% of my portfolio in two very similar companies.

This also applies to the market. Because of its top-heavy nature, it has become almost like an AI-ETF, as I have joked in prior articles. The hyperscalers that dominate the index may all be unique, yet they are all trying to win big in what I expect to become a “winner takes most” AI industry.

That’s also one of my biggest criticisms and a reason why I prefer the equal-weight S&P 500 (RSP) over the market-weight S&P 500 (the “normal” S&P 500). So far, this year, that thesis is gaining momentum, as the RSP/SPY ratio below shows:

I would even make the case that this year, so far, has been a wake-up call for many younger investors who may have been massively overweight in software and related tech stocks. When these started to crack due to disruption fears, a lot of investors saw their portfolios crater.

Moreover, on top of my belief that I can reasonably diversify with just a few positions, I also like to go big into my favorite ideas. Although I could easily go to 40 or even 50 holdings based on my long watchlist, I decided against buying everything I like to put the most emphasis on my favorite ideas. I always base this on at least four questions.

  1. What is the main driver of returns?
  2. Why does it compound?
  3. What could break my thesis?
  4. Is this worth a top-5 weighting, or is it just a stock I like?

This may sound super obvious, but a high concentration needs to be earned. My top-five picks are all companies that I expect can help me reach my financial goals on their own.

In other words, if just one of these companies delivers on its long-term potential, it could meaningfully change my financial future.

That’s how much I trust them.

What I Am Looking For In A Stock

I already briefly mentioned four key questions I ask before I invest.

The answers to these questions are based on a scorecard that looks for a few key things, including business model durability. I do not want to own a company in my top holdings that I have owned for just a few quarters. For example, that’s why I do not invest in semiconductor stocks but in landowners like Texas Pacific Land, where AI data centers are a bonus, not a must-have for the thesis.

I also need pricing power. Inflation is one of my favorite topics for a reason, as I not only expect inflation to accelerate, but also because on a long-term basis, every pricing power benefit can yield big alpha.

This is one of the reasons why I do not own PepsiCo (PEP) anymore. As much as I like PEP for dividend investors, given my age (30), its growth profile was simply not strong enough. I also did not want any more exposure to the ongoing fight for shelf space at global supermarkets. It’s tough. PEP may be a great company (I’m bullish on it), but I want more pricing power.

That’s also why I focused on TransDigm (TDG), as this aerospace supplier has more pricing power than a defense contractor that deals directly with the Department of War.

Then there’s the reinvestment runway. I added this to my scorecard this year, as it’s a critical concept. For example, one of the indicators I care about is ROIC. That’s the return on invested capital. It measures how high the payoff is of company investments. However, a high ROIC isn’t enough. Altria (MO), for example, has one of the highest ROICs on the entire market (see below).

However, because smoking (it sells Marlboros among other products) is in a secular decline, there is not a lot of room to invest. That’s why it has such a high yield, as most of its earnings are returned to shareholders.

Old Dominion Freight Line has a yield of less than 1% and a low-double-digit payout ratio. That’s because most of its capital is invested in new trucks and valuable real estate (ODFL owns almost all of its distribution centers in very strategic locations).

Next up is balance sheet health. Although I have bought companies with credit ratings below BBB- (BBB- is the first investment-grade rating), I never buy companies that cannot withstand steep downturns. When I was younger, I invested in turnarounds, especially in oil and gas. Nowadays, I make no exceptions. Zero.

Then there’s another idea, which is optionality. That’s a fancy way of saying that I want companies that have more than one way to win. For example, Texas Pacific Land and LandBridge have tailwinds from oil and gas production royalties (both volumes and pricing), the booming water business, and potential data center deals on their surface land.

Last but not least, I want good management ( management I trust) and a valuation that offers attractive upside. As this may sound obvious as well, I have one thing to mention here, which is that valuations need to be taken with a grain of salt. Texas Pacific, for example, usually trades somewhere between 30 and 40x earnings, which is 50% to 100% above the current market valuation. However:

  • It has no debt.
  • It has a top-3 net margin profile in the S&P 500.
  • It has no major CapEx requirements.

In other words, no capital is wasted on interest payments, a big share of revenue is turned into earnings, and, because of no major CapEx, most of it ends up as free cash flow.

This brings me to the next part of this article.

Treat It As A Case-Study

Sometimes, people tell me they do like my stock ideas but avoid them because they simply don’t fit their strategy. These comments make me very happy, as it’s not my goal to push anyone into specific stocks. My goal is to provide food for thought.

That’s why I will now quickly walk you through my picks. However, while I will tell you why I like them, I will mainly tell you what the lesson here is for further research.

Texas Pacific Land & LandBridge

These two companies are very similar, as they both own land in the Permian Basin. That’s America’s biggest oil basin. They own mineral, water, and surface rights, which means they make money on everything that happens on their land and under the surface. There are no similar companies on the stock market while I am writing this.

As a result, they make money from oil and gas royalties without having to spend a dime on CapEx, as investments are made by actual oil companies like Chevron (CVX). Moreover, they have water rights, which means they can supply water for the drilling process. On top of that, they make money from produced water, which is an oil and gas by-product that needs to be either recycled or pumped back into the ground.

The cherry on top is that the surface business is increasingly booming, as data centers have started to figure out that West Texas (that’s where the Permian is located) comes with dirt-cheap power, plenty of undeveloped land, no stakeholders like heavily populated communities, and few regulations.

LandBridge has one data center company looking for a spot on its land. Texas Pacific has ownership in Bolt Data & Energy, a company founded by Eric Schmidt. Although I would still be bullish on TPL and LB if they were never to get a data center on their land, I am convinced that both will get data centers in the years ahead.

So, what’s the lesson?

  • I love the high-margin royalty business because high margins translate into plenty of opportunities to reward shareholders and grow wealth.
  • Companies with low CapEx needs tend to enjoy higher valuations.
  • Companies with a lot of optionality tend to thrive due to their ability to capture multiple tailwinds.

Old Dominion Freight Line

ODFL operates in the volatile less-than-truckload industry. That’s different from truckload, as trucks don’t drive for single customers from Point A to Point B. LTL is based on shorter distances. Trucks pick up goods from multiple customers, bring them to service centers where they are sorted and put on other trucks to be sent to their final destination.

Essentially, LTL is based on efficiently connecting a wide range of (mostly industrial) customers. While this is a very cyclical business, LTL companies can actually create competitive advantages. ODFL has done this thanks to the most efficient network in the entire LTL industry. ODFL has spent decades buying high-quality land in dense areas, which means it owns most of its real estate outright.

It also put a big emphasis on service quality. For more than a decade, ODFL has been the number one in service quality. This has provided it with pricing power.

Essentially, it built a network that paid off, allowing it to invest even more. That flywheel made it one of the most successful industrial stocks in history, as dramatic as that may sound.

ODFL is cyclical, but I like that fact. I buy it when manufacturing sentiment (i.e., the ISM Manufacturing Index) is weak. During a phase of a boom, I just hold it. Then, during weakness, I buy more.

The main lesson, to me, is:

  • Cyclical companies don’t make for bad investments as long as they are able to grow over time and generate shareholder value. If they fail to do that, they are nothing more than a cyclical trading vehicle. I don’t care for those stocks.
  • Companies with discipline can even grow when demand is weak. ODFL has used its sales team aggressively to win new deals in recent years. That’s why I expect the next upswing to be violent (in a positive sense).
  • Real estate is often a strong advantage if used properly.

Carlisle Companies

Carlisle is a construction supplier. It mainly produces supplies for the remodeling and maintenance of commercial buildings. It is an industry leader with an ROIC of nearly 30.0% and an adjusted margin of slightly more than 26.0%. Even better, it has a lot of options for future investments, as it uses M&A to add new products in growth markets like insulation.

This is a terrific business to be in, as more than 55% of commercial buildings are older than 35 years. This is an age where aggressive spending is needed to prolong the lifespan of a building. CSL services that market. In recent years, it saw headwinds from elevated rates and a housing/commercial market that was stuck.

Now, as it gets unstuck, massive secular pent-up demand is likely to hit its order books.

Moreover, in its 4Q25, it stuck to its Vision 2030 goals, which include at least $40 in EPS. At 20x earnings, this implies an $800 stock price, nearly twice its current price. While I’m not guaranteeing that, I believe CSL equity is way too cheap.

The biggest takeaways here are:

  • ROICs often beat “cheap” P/E ratios, as CSL shines when it comes to putting capital to work, and it has plenty of room to spend capital.
  • CSL is the result of massive divestitures, resulting in a full focus on its core business. It’s one of the reasons why I avoid conglomerates.
  • Reinvesting capital is often a better use of capital than high dividend payments.

QXO Inc.

QXO is one of my latest additions. It’s a new company founded by Brad Jacobs, the same billionaire who also founded XPO (XPO), United Rentals (URI), and other businesses that ended up changing the industries they operate in.

Essentially, his gameplan is straightforward, as he buys companies in fragmented industries. He then uses technology to improve them, boost cash flows, and buy more peers. QXO is his latest endeavour, this time in the building distribution industry. It’s a major customer of Carlisle Companies after it bought Beacon Roofing. That was its first big deal.

Now, it aims for $50 billion in annual revenues over the next decade, a goal that could be supported by its latest move, which is the announcement to buy Kodiak Building Partners, a company that I consider a perfect fit due to its footprint in roofing, waterproofing, and other areas that QXO already operates in.

Although QXO is super volatile and an experiment, I feel so good about it, as I have spent almost a decade learning about Brad Jacobs. I also know that this is one of the best spaces to use AI as a disruptor. QXO sees low-hanging fruit in a massively fragmented industry that mostly consists of older, private companies.

While QXO is riskier than my other picks due to execution risks, I feel really good holding this company. It also shows just how effective a strong M&A strategy can be, which Brad Jacobs has proven for decades.

Other M&A giants I like are Danaher (DHR) in healthcare, TransDigm (TDG) in aerospace (I own it), Amphenol (APH) in the industrial space, and Intercontinental Exchange (ICE) in the stock exchanges industry.

Now, before this article gets way too long, here’s my takeaway:

Takeaway

I have a highly concentrated portfolio because I believe in putting a lot of emphasis on high-conviction ideas. My top-five holdings represent my best ideas and my biggest theses, including energy, housing, secular growth in data centers, produced water, commercial remodeling needs, and AI disruption in “unexpected” places like construction distribution.

In my case, it’s not about being too risky, but about allocating capital to the companies that I believe have the best risk/reward to allow me to achieve my financial goals as fast as possible.

In fact, I am so bullish that I believe even one or two of these companies can allow me to achieve my targets over the next few decades.

However, for now, the biggest takeaway is the uniqueness that these companies bring to the table. We can use this in future stock research, as they all bring something to the table that we should look for in other investments, including high ROICs, competent management, a lot of room for aggressive investments (tied to ROIC), secular growth, and even optionality.

Needless to say, the goal wasn’t to get anyone to copy me. This is food for thought. In general, I have to say that my way of running a portfolio is unorthodox and should not be copied by most people, especially not risk-averse people who like to sleep well at night. Please keep that in mind.

data.title: Chart Of The Day 2/13/26: 'Flash Crashes' Still Plaguing This Market
data.description:
By Mike Larson

It happened again yesterday. A silver “Flash Crash” that sent the metal down 11% in minutes. Gold suffered a similar (though smaller) slump, while equities sold off all day long. That volatility is keeping traders on edge... and rightfully so.

Take a look at the MoneyShow Chart of the Day. It shows the intraday trading action in spot Bitcoin and silver, gold, and S&P 500 E-mini futures yesterday morning and early afternoon. You can see that this was a cross-asset-class move, even as the percentage declines were smaller outside of silver.

Bitcoin, Silver, S&P 500 E-minis, Gold (Thursday Intraday Chart)

(Source: TradingView)

What happened? Was it a broader “risk-out” move? Part of a big rotation trade into bonds? A reaction to recent AI-driven washouts in SaaS stocks and financial services providers, plus yesterday's wipeout in trucking names?

I didn’t see any great explanations myself. But I do know one thing: seemingly unprovoked sudden, violent moves in any asset class can shake confidence. When you see them in multiple markets, that’s even more concerning.

I’ve been saying for a while that the first quarter of 2026 - and maybe the entire first half - will be choppier than we've gotten used to. So have MoneyShow experts like Jeff Hirsch. With market action now validating that forecast, be sure to account for it in your trading plan!

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Originally published on MoneyShow.com
